frames.caption: result = 0; reverse the 8-bit slice of n = 00000001 by pouring n's low bit into result, 8 times.
frames.note: We rebuild the answer one bit at a time from the BOTTOM up. The trick is that the first low bit we read is written deepest in result, then shoved upward by every later pass — so the input's least-significant bit becomes the result's most-significant bit. The 32-bit problem is this same loop run 32 times; we show 8 bits so the mirror is legible.

frames.caption: i=0: n's low bit is 1. result = (00000000 << 1) | 1 appends it to result's bottom slot.
frames.note: One pass does two moves at once. result << 1 slides everything already collected up by one position, opening a fresh 0 in result's LOWEST slot; (n & 1) reads n's LOWEST bit; the OR drops that bit into the slot just opened. So the bit that was at the BOTTOM of the input is written to the BOTTOM of result first — and then keeps getting pushed UP on every later pass, which is exactly what reverses the order.

frames.caption: result = 00000001 — bit 1 now sits at the bottom; earlier bits shifted up one place.
frames.note: Watch the single 1: it starts at input bit 0 (array slot 7). On pass 0 it is the bit appended to result's bit 0; each of the remaining 7 passes shifts result left once, lifting that 1 one position higher, so it ends at bit 7 (array slot 0). Input slot i mirrors to output slot 7 - i — bit 0 swaps with bit 7, the definition of reversing.

frames.caption: n >>= 1 drops the consumed bit -> n = 00000000; the next-lower bit slides into the bit-0 slot.
frames.note: After harvesting n's low bit we discard it with n >>= 1, sliding the next-lowest bit down into the bit-0 slot so the next pass reads it. Working in uint makes this a LOGICAL right shift (zero-filled): a signed >> would sign-extend a 1 forever and the loop would never see n reach 0.

frames.caption: Loop done: 8-bit reverse of 00000001 is 10000000 (the 1 moved bit 0 -> bit 7). In 32-bit code, 1u reverses to 2147483648.
frames.note: Each of the W passes shifted result left once and appended one input bit, so after W passes input bit k is sitting at result bit W-1-k — every bit mirrored across the center. The full LC 190 loop is the identical idea over 32 bits, costing exactly 32 iterations: O(1) time, O(1) space.
